question: What information must the controller provide to the data subject at the time when personal data are obtained, according to Article 13?
gold_answer: The controller must provide the data subject with the identity and contact details of the controller and, where applicable, of the controller's representative; the contact details of the data protection officer, where applicable; the purposes of the processing for which the personal data are intended as well as the legal basis for the processing; where the processing is based on point (f) of Article 6(1), the legitimate interests pursued by the controller or by a third party; the recipients or categories of recipients of the personal data, if any; where applicable, the fact that the controller intends to transfer personal data to a third country or international organisation and the existence or absence of an adequacy decision by the Commission, or in the case of transfers referred to in Article 46 or 47, or the second subparagraph of Article 49(1), reference to the appropriate or suitable safeguards and the means by which to obtain a copy of them or where they have been made available. In addition, the controller must provide the data subject with the period for which the personal data will be stored, or if that is not possible, the criteria used to determine that period; the existence of the right to request from the controller access to and rectification or erasure of personal data or restriction of processing concerning the data subject or to object to processing as well as the right to data portability; where the processing is based on point (a) of Article 6(1) or point (a) of Article 9(2), the existence of the right to withdraw consent at any time, without affecting the lawfulness of processing based on consent before its withdrawal; the right to lodge a complaint with a supervisory authority; whether the provision of personal data is a statutory or contractual requirement, or a requirement necessary to enter into a contract, as well as whether the data subject is obliged to provide the personal data and of the possible consequences of failure to provide such data; the existence of automated decision-making, including profiling, referred to in Article 22(1) and (4) and, at least in those cases, meaningful information about the logic involved, as well as the significance and the envisaged consequences of such processing for the data subject.
llm_response: {'answer': 'At the time personal data are obtained, the controller must provide the data subject with: identity and contact details of the controller (and representative, if any); purposes and legal basis of processing; recipients of the data; storage period or criteria; rights to access, rectify, erase, restrict, object, and data portability; right to withdraw consent; right to lodge a complaint; existence of automated decision-making and its consequences; whether providing data is mandatory and consequences of non-provision; and details of international transfers if applicable (Article 13, points 1 and 2).', 'full_answer': "According to Article 13, when personal data are collected directly from the data subject, the controller must provide a comprehensive set of information to ensure fair and transparent processing. This includes: (1) identity and contact details of the controller (and representative if applicable); (2) purposes and legal basis for processing; (3) recipients of the data; (4) storage period or criteria for determining it; (5) the data subject's rights (access, rectification, erasure, restriction, objection, data portability); (6) the right to withdraw consent; (7) the right to lodge a complaint with a supervisory authority; (8) information about automated decision-making and profiling, including the logic and consequences; (9) whether providing data is a statutory or contractual requirement and the consequences of not providing it; and (10) details of any international transfers of data, including safeguards. These requirements are set out in Article 13, points 1 and 2, and are mandatory unless the data subject already has the information.", 'key_snippet': 'Where personal data relating to a data subject are collected from the data subject, the controller shall, at the time when personal data are obtained, provide the data subject with all of the following information:', 'chunks': ['chapter III, article 13, point 1', 'chapter III, article 13, point 2'], 'percentage': ['50', '50']}

question: What is the subject matter of Article 1 of the GDPR Regulation?
gold_answer: Article 1 lays down rules relating to the protection of natural persons with regard to the processing of personal data and rules relating to the free movement of personal data.
llm_response: {'answer': "Article 1 of the GDPR sets out the Regulation's subject matter: it lays down rules for the protection of natural persons regarding the processing of personal data, and rules for the free movement of such data within the Union. It also states that the Regulation protects fundamental rights and freedoms, especially the right to data protection, and that the free movement of personal data shall not be restricted for these reasons.", 'full_answer': 'According to the evidence, Article 1 of the GDPR addresses two core aspects: (1) the establishment of rules for the protection of natural persons in relation to the processing of personal data, and (2) the establishment of rules for the free movement of personal data within the European Union. It further clarifies that the Regulation is intended to protect fundamental rights and freedoms, particularly the right to the protection of personal data. Additionally, Article 1 states that the free movement of personal data shall not be restricted or prohibited on grounds related to the protection of natural persons with regard to processing. These points are explicitly stated in the first three points of Article 1, which form the foundational scope and purpose of the GDPR.', 'key_snippet': 'This Regulation lays down rules relating to the protection of natural persons with regard to the processing of personal data and rules relating to the free movement of personal data.', 'chunks': ['chapter I, article 1, point 1', 'chapter I, article 1, point 2', 'chapter I, article 1, point 3'], 'percentage': ['50', '30', '20']}

question: What is the material scope of Article 2 of the GDPR Regulation?
gold_answer: Article 2 of the GDPR Regulation applies to the processing of personal data wholly or partly by automated means and to the processing other than by automated means of personal data which form part of a filing system or are intended to form part of a filing system. It does not apply to the processing of personal data in the course of an activity which falls outside the scope of Union law, by the Member States when carrying out activities which fall within the scope of Chapter 2 of Title V of the TEU, by a natural person in the course of a purely personal or household activity, or by competent authorities for the purposes of the prevention, investigation, detection or prosecution of criminal offences or the execution of criminal penalties, including the safeguarding against and the prevention of threats to public security.
llm_response: {'answer': 'Article 2 of the GDPR defines its material scope by stating that the Regulation applies to the processing of personal data wholly or partly by automated means, and to non-automated processing if the data form part of a filing system or are intended to form part of one. It also lists specific exclusions, such as processing by a natural person in a purely personal or household context, or for certain public security and criminal law purposes.', 'full_answer': "Article 2(1) establishes the general material scope of the GDPR: it applies to processing of personal data 'wholly or partly by automated means' and to processing 'other than by automated means of personal data which form part of a filing system or are intended to form part of a filing system.' This ensures broad coverage of both digital and structured manual records. However, Article 2(2) lists exclusions, such as processing by natural persons for purely personal or household activities, processing for certain public security and criminal law purposes, and processing by EU institutions under separate rules. question: Under what circumstances can Union or Member State law restrict the scope of the obligations and rights provided for in Articles 12 to 22 and Article 34, as well as Article 5?
gold_answer: Union or Member State law may restrict the scope of the obligations and rights provided for in Articles 12 to 22 and Article 34, as well as Article 5, when such a restriction respects the essence of the fundamental rights and freedoms and is a necessary and proportionate measure in a democratic society to safeguard national security, defence, public security, the prevention, investigation, detection or prosecution of criminal offences or the execution of criminal penalties, including the safeguarding against and the prevention of threats to public security, other important objectives of general public interest of the Union or of a Member State, in particular an important economic or financial interest of the Union or of a Member State, including monetary, budgetary and taxation a matters, public health and social security, the protection of judicial independence and judicial proceedings, the prevention, investigation, detection and prosecution of breaches of ethics for regulated professions, a monitoring, inspection or regulatory function connected, even occasionally, to the exercise of official authority in the cases referred to in points (a) to (e) and (g), the protection of the data subject or the rights and freedoms of others, or the enforcement of civil law claims.
llm_response: {'answer': 'Union or Member State law can restrict the scope of obligations and rights under Articles 12 to 22 and Article 5 under specific circumstances. Additionally, professional secrecy regulated by Union or Member State law, including statutory obligations, can restrict data processing.', 'full_answer': 'The scope of obligations and rights detailed in Articles 12 to 22 and Article 5 can be restricted by Union or Member State law under several conditions. These conditions are primarily for ensuring compliance with legal obligations that the data controller must adhere to, as stipulated in Union or Member State law. They also serve to protect the rights and legitimate interests of other natural or legal persons, or when there is an important public interest of the Union or a Member State. Another specific circumstance for restriction is when the personal data must be kept confidential due to a professional secrecy obligation regulated by Union or Member State law (chunk ID: 9.564).
